coredata.dc:title: Dual-emission fluorescence detection of histidine using carbon dots and calcein/Ni2+ complexes
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Jinshui
Song, Bo
Fu, Ting
coredata.dc:description: Histidine (His) is a natural amino acid that plays very important roles in biota. However, the low concentrations of His in biological fluids and the similar structures and properties of other amino acids mean it is difficult to selectively determine His concentrations in biological fluids with a high degree of sensitivity. A novel ratiometric fluorescence probe for detecting His in aqueous solutions is described here. The method involves carbon dots (CDs) and calcein/Ni2+ complexes. At an excitation wavelength of 480 nm, the CD/calcein system emits green fluorescence (maximum emission from calcein at 512 nm) and red fluorescence (maximum emission from CDs at 617 nm). The presence of Ni2+ decreases the calcein fluorescence intensity because of static quenching caused by the formation of calcein/Ni2+ complexes but the CD fluorescence intensity remains almost unchanged. Fluorescence of calcein/Ni2+ complexes provides the response, and the presence of His binds to Ni2+ via cooperative chelation and produces free calcein causing fluorescence to be recovered. CDs provide a self-calibration fluorescence signal, the intensity of which remains almost unchanged in the presence of His. The ratio of the fluorescence intensities at 512 and 617 nm (I 512/I 617) was strongly related to the His concentration in the range 0.5–22 μM, and the detection limit was 0.16 μM. The specificity of Ni2+/His interactions allows His to be determined without interference from other species. The method was successfully used to determine His in diluted human urine. The recovery was acceptable, suggesting that the biosensor can be used to determine His in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescence
Carbon dots
Histidine
Calcein

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanism involved in fluorescence detection of His

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province, China (1708085MB48).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Transmission electron microscopy images of the CDs. (b) Fourier-transform infrared spectra for CDs.

(a) Fluorescence spectral changes of calcein/CDs system mixed with various concentration of Ni2+ (0–8 μM). (b) Fluorescent response of CD/calcein/Ni2+ probe system containing His concentrations of 0–22 μM.
(a) The CIE chromaticity coordinates of CD/calcein/Ni2+ at different contents of His (0–22 μM). (b) The linearity of I 512/I 617 emission intensity ratio to increasing His concentration (0–22 μM).

The fluorescence intensity ratios I 512/I 617 of CD/calcein/Ni2+ probe system in aqueous solutions containing 100 μM interfering substances (grey bars) and the subsequent addition of 10 μM His (red bars).
Schematic of the CD/calcein/Ni2+ system for determining His.
Results for the analysis of His in human urine samples.

head.ce:title: Dual-emission fluorescence detection of histidine using carbon dots and calcein/Ni2+ complexes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Anhui Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing, The Key Laboratory of Functional Molecular Solids, Ministry of Education, Anhui Laboratory of Molecule-based Materials, Anhui Normal University, Wuhu 241000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Anhui Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing, The Key Laboratory of Functional Molecular Solids, Ministry of Education, Anhui Laboratory of Molecule-based Materials, Anhui Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Anhui Key Laboratory of Chemo/Biosensing, The Key Laboratory of Functional Molecular Solids, Ministry of Education, Anhui Laboratory of Molecule-based Materials, Anhui Normal University, Wuhu 241000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the ratiometric fluorometric method for determining histidine (His) based on carbon dots coupled with calcein/Ni2+ complexes.

Histidine (His) is a natural amino acid that plays very important roles in biota. However, the low concentrations of His in biological fluids and the similar structures and properties of other amino acids mean it is difficult to selectively determine His concentrations in biological fluids with a high degree of sensitivity. A novel ratiometric fluorescence probe for detecting His in aqueous solutions is described here. The method involves carbon dots (CDs) and calcein/Ni2+ complexes. At an excitation wavelength of 480 nm, the CD/calcein system emits green fluorescence (maximum emission from calcein at 512 nm) and red fluorescence (maximum emission from CDs at 617 nm). The presence of Ni2+ decreases the calcein fluorescence intensity because of static quenching caused by the formation of calcein/Ni2+ complexes but the CD fluorescence intensity remains almost unchanged. Fluorescence of calcein/Ni2+ complexes provides the response, and the presence of His binds to Ni2+ via cooperative chelation and produces free calcein causing fluorescence to be recovered. CDs provide a self-calibration fluorescence signal, the intensity of which remains almost unchanged in the presence of His. The ratio of the fluorescence intensities at 512 and 617 nm (I 512/I 617) was strongly related to the His concentration in the range 0.5–22 μM, and the detection limit was 0.16 μM. The specificity of Ni2+/His interactions allows His to be determined without interference from other species. The method was successfully used to determine His in diluted human urine. The recovery was acceptable, suggesting that the biosensor can be used to determine His in real samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Amino acids are essential components of biological materials and are involved in various phenomena. Histidine (His), which is a natural amino acid, contains an imidazole moiety and is an important neuromodulator and neurotransmitter in the central nervous systems of mammals. His prevents micro-traumatic internal hemorrhage and controls the transport of metals associated with biological bases [1]. Excess His may cause asthma, schizophrenia, and thrombosis, and chronic His deficiency may cause epilepsy, failure of normal erythropoiesis development, Friedreich ataxia, and Parkinson’s disease [2,3]. The His index of urine is clinically important for monitoring metabolic disorders and “histamine sepsis” in humans. However, it is difficult to sensitively and selectively determine His in biological samples because of the low concentrations of His and the similar properties and structures of other species, including other amino acids.

A number of analytical methods involving capillary electrophoresis, electrochemical voltammetry, fluorometry, high-performance liquid chromatography, and UV–vis spectrophotometry have been developed for determining His [3,4]. Fluorescent probes are particularly useful for determining the concentrations of trace metal ions and biomolecules because they are convenient to use, give instant responses, are effectively selective, and give good sensitivities [5,6]. Carbon dots (CDs), which are the most promising fluorescent probes, have recently been attracting great attention because they are easy to prepare and cost-effective and have excellent photostabilities [7,8]. Element scarcity does not need to be considered and complicated, tedious, cost-sensitive, and even unacceptable preparation steps are not required when synthesizing CDs, unlike when synthesizing some other fluorescent nanomaterials [9,10]. Most fluorescent nanosensors have single-wavelength detection modes and involve either “turn-on” or “turn-off” strategies. The effectiveness of such a fluorescent nanosensor can be negatively affected by experimental conditions such as the optical path length, instrument used, environmental factors, and autofluorescence [11]. These effects limit the sensitivity and accuracy when using probes [12,13]. Ratiometric fluorescent probes have two separate emission bands. The presence of an analyte will markedly change the intensities of these emission bands. The ratiometric strategy avoids potential interferences caused by background fluorescence and improves the sensitivity and accuracy relative to using a single signal strategy [14–16]. Ratiometric fluorescent His probes based on N,S,P-CD/(N-acetyl-L-cysteine functionalized gold nanocluster–Cu2+) complexes have recently been developed [17]. However, Cu2+ can bind to His, cysteine, and glutathione, so selectively determining His requires N-ethylmaleimide (which is toxic and unstable) to be used [18,19]. This inevitably limits the applications of such a biosensor. Amino acids have very similar structures, and many numerous amino acids will normally be present in a biological sample, so it is very difficult to selectively determine an individual amino acid using a simple method [20]. Ni2+ has a strong affinity for His, and this has allowed metal ion affinity chromatography to be used to determine His in biological samples [21]. To the best of our knowledge, ratiometric fluorescent probes based on Ni2+/His affinity pairs have not previously been used to determine His.

In this study, a novel ratiometric fluorescence probe based on CD/calcein/Ni2+ complexes was developed for determining His in aqueous solutions. Fluorescence of the calcein/Ni2+ complex provided the response signal, and fluorescence of the complex was enhanced in the presence of His. The CDs provided a self-calibration signal, the fluorescence intensity of which remained almost the same in the presence or absence of His. The ratio between the fluorescence intensities of calcein and CDs was used to determine the His concentration.
The chemicals and apparatus used are described in Electronic Supporting Material.

Citric acid (0.0021 g) and p-phenylenediamine (0.0216 g) were added to 30 mL of H2O, then the mixture was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated to 180 °C for 10 h. When the reaction was complete, the Teflon-sealed autoclave was allowed to cool naturally in air. A dark brown solution was produced. The solution of CDs that had been produced was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 15 min, then the supernatant was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight 500 Da) for 24 h to purify the CDs. The solution containing the CDs was then freeze-dried to give the solid CDs.

The effect of His on fluorescence of the CD/calcein/Ni2+ system was investigated by mixing 100 μL of a solution containing as-prepared CDs at a concentration of 0.85 g L−1, 200 μL of 1 μM calcein, and 60 μL of 0.1 mM Ni2+ with 100 μL of a solution containing His. This was performed using solutions containing different concentrations of His. The mixture was diluted to 1000 μL with Tris–HCl buffer (0.05 M, pH 7.9) and then thoroughly shaken. The mixture was then incubated at room temperature for 25 min. The fluorescence spectrum was then acquired using a fluorescence spectrometer with the excitation wavelength of 480 nm. The ratio of the fluorescence intensities at 512 and 617 nm (I 512/I 617) was calculated and used to quantify His.

The ability of the ratiometric fluorescence probe to determine His in human urine samples was evaluated. Urine samples were collected from healthy adult volunteers. Each sample was passed through a 0.22 mm membrane filter and then diluted by a factor of 20 before analysis. Each sample was spiked with His and then analyzed using the method described above. The experiment was performed in compliance with the relevant laws and institutional guidelines.

The CDs were readily synthesized using a solvothermal method using citric acid and p-phenylenediamine as precursors. The morphologies of the as-prepared CDs were investigated by transmission electron microscopy. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the CDs were uniformly spherical and well dispersed in the aqueous solution. The particle size distribution was narrow (the range of diameters was 5–11 nm) and the mean diameter was 8 nm (Fig. 1a). The surface functional groups and chemical compositions of the CDs were investigated by Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), respectively. The Fourier-transform infrared spectrum is shown in Fig. 1b. The wide absorption band at 3209–3421 cm−1 was ascribed to O–H/N–H stretching vibrations, and the absorption peak at 2921 cm−1 was attributed to C–H stretching vibrations [22]. The absorption band centered on 1626 cm−1 was assigned to C=O stretching vibration. The sharp peaks at 1513 cm−1 were attributed to N–H stretching vibrations [23]. The narrow band between 1290 and 1407 cm−1 was ascribed to characteristic C–N stretching vibrations. The absorption band at 995–1170 cm−1 was attributed to C–O bending vibrations [24]. The peak at 831 cm−1 was attributed to C–N–H bending vibrations. These results indicated that the surfaces of the as-prepared CDs were particularly rich in amino and hydroxy functional groups, making the CDs very soluble in water [22]. XPS analysis was performed to further investigate the chemical compositions of the CDs. It can be seen from Fig. S1 in supplementary information that the three main element peaks for the CDs were C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s. As shown in Fig. S2 in supplementary information, deconvoluting the C 1s band gave peaks at 284.3, 284.9, 285.5, and 286.8 eV, which were ascribed to C=C, C–C, C–N/C–O, and C=O/C=N, respectively. As shown in Fig. S3 in supplementary information, the N 1s peak was resolved into peaks at 399, 399.5, and 400.2 eV, which were ascribed to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and amino N, respectively. As shown in Fig. S4 in supplementary information, the O 1s XPS spectrum contained peaks at 532.5 and 531.3 eV, which were ascribed to C–OH/C–O–C and C=O bonds, respectively [23]. The XPS results were consistent with the Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism involved in fluorescence detection of His
ce:section.ce:para: The strategy used to determine His using the CD/calcein/Ni2+ probe is shown in Scheme 1 . Calcein provides the output signal and the CDs provide an internal reference signal. Adding Ni2+ effectively quenches the yellow–green fluorescence of calcein at around 512 nm produced using an excitation wavelength of 480 nm but does not markedly affect the red fluorescence of CDs at around 617 nm also produced at an excitation wavelength of 480 nm (Fig. 2 a). Strong coordination/chelation interactions between the carboxyl groups in calcein and Ni2+ cause non-radiative electron transfer, which causes marked fluorescence quenching at 512 nm. The quenching mechanism was investigated by making fluorescence lifetime measurements. As shown in Fig. S5 in supplementary information, the fluorescence emission decay curves and lifetimes for the system were not markedly different before and after Ni2+ was added. This indicated that static quenching occurred [25]. However, adding His to the CD/calcein/Ni2+ system caused yellow–green fluorescence to be recovered because the His would have bound to Ni2+ through cooperative chelation of Ni2+ to carboxyl and imidazole moieties in His. This would have removed Ni2+ ions from the calcein/Ni2+ complexes and produced free calcein, causing fluorescence to be recovered, as shown in Scheme 1. The feasibility of using the CD/calcein/Ni2+ probe to determine His was investigated by first adding His to a CD/calcein system. As shown in Fig. S6 in supplementary information, His did not markedly change the fluorescence intensity of the CD/calcein system. This indicated that His alone did not affect the optical properties of the CD/calcein system. The presence of His enhances fluorescence of the CD/calcein/Ni2+ system at 512 nm because His becomes bound to Ni2+ and causes the Ni2+ to become dissociated from calcein/Ni2+ complexes. This mechanism is similar to the mechanism involved in His remove Ni2+ from dyes-Ni2+ complex that was described in a previous publication [26]. The CDs provided the self-calibration signal, whose fluorescence intensity remained almost unchanged when in the presence of His. The CD/calcein/Ni2+ probe could therefore be used to determine His because His caused the fluorescence of the CD/calcein/Ni2+ system to be recovered. The ratio between the fluorescence intensities of calcein and CDs could therefore be used to quantify His.

The method for determining His was optimized by performing tests using different pH values and reaction times. First, the ability of the system to determine 10 µM His was tested using solutions at pH 7.1, 7.3, 7.5, 7.7, 7.9, 8.1, 8.3, 8.5, 8.7, and 8.9. As shown in Fig. S7 in supplementary information, the (I 512/I 617) − (I 512/I 617)0 response increased gradually as the pH increased from 7.1 to 7.9 ((I 512/I 617)0 and (I 512/I 617) are the fluorescence intensity ratios of the CD/calcein/Ni2+ system in the absence and presence of 10 μM His, respectively). However, the (I 512/I 617) − (I 512/I 617)0 response decreased when the pH was increased from 7.9 to 8.9. This indicated that a weakly alkaline medium at pH 7.9 was optimal for determining His using the CD/calcein/Ni2+ system. The incubation time is very important when performing rapid sensing procedures. The results acquired using different incubation times are shown in Fig. S8 in supplementary information. The I 512/I 617 ratio increased dramatically for 25 min after His was added. The fluorescence intensity was therefore determined after incubating a mixture for 25 min in the subsequent tests.

The CD/calcein/Ni2+ ratiometric probe was used to determine His using the optimized conditions. As shown in Fig. 2b, the fluorescence intensity at 512 nm increased as the His concentration increased but the CD fluorescence intensity at 617 nm was almost unaffected as the His concentration increased. The Commission Internationale de l’Éclairage coordinates for CD/calcein/Ni2+ fluorescence determined from emission spectra acquired at different His concentrations indicated that the CD/calcein/Ni2+ fluorescence color covered the range red to yellow–green (Fig. 3 a). As shown in Fig. 3b, the I 512/I 617 ratio was linearly related to the His concentration in the range 0–22 μM. The linear regression equation describing the relationship was Y = 0.1454 X + 0.2103 (R2 = 0.998), where Y is the I 512/I 617 ratio and X is the His concentration (μM). The limit of detection, defined as 3σ/k (where σ is the standard deviation of the results for eight replicate analyses of a blank solution and k is the slope of the calibration curve) was 0.16 μM [27]. It can be seen from Table S1 in supplementary information that the ratiometric analysis method described here gave comparable results to most previously published methods [17,28–33]. Moreover, the developed ratiometric fluorescence method shows some attractive advantages, including high sensitivity, a quick response, and easy operation. Ratiometric methods can involve self-calibration and are therefore more robust to interferences and give more accurate than many other methods. The method described here therefore has great promise for use as a biomedical assay. The normal His concentrations in urine and serum are 1.8 × 10−4 to 1.2 × 10−3 M and 8.6 × 10−5 to 1.1 × 10−4 M, respectively, but His concentrations in serum in people with histidinemia disease can be between 2.9 × 10−4 and 1.42 × 10−3 M [34]. The limit of detection of the biosensing system for His makes the system suitable for determining His concentrations in urine and serum, including serum from people with histidinemia disease. The CD/calcein/Ni2+ system is therefore an ideal probe for the ratiometric determination of His. The calcein/Ni2+ complex is used to detect His and the CDs act as reference components.

The specificity of the CD/calcein/Ni2+ probe for His was assessed by evaluating the effects of other amino acids and potential coexisting biomolecules on the His concentrations determined using the probe. The concentrations of the potentially interfering substances and His were 100 and 10 μM, respectively. The I 512/I 617 fluorescence intensity ratio was much higher for His than the other substances (grey bars), and negligible changes in the I 512/I 617 fluorescence intensity ratio were found when the other biomolecules were present (grey bars), as shown in Fig. 4 . Meanwhile, the anti-interference capability of the probe was also investigated by adding a mixture consisting of His and other potential interferences into the system (red bars). Little influence on the I 512/I 617 fluorescence intensity ratio was observed compared to that on the system in which only His existed. The good selectivity of the probe is caused by His containing both an imidazole group and an amino group in close proximity, meaning stable Ni2+/His complexes can form. Some metal ions can interact with more than one amino acid, but Ni2+ only has a strong affinity for His [32]. The CD/calcein/Ni2+ biosensor can therefore distinguish between His and other amino acids, including cysteine, without the need for an additional masking agent. This is a desirable characteristic for a biosensor.

The influence of Cu2+ on the detection of His was also investigated. In the presence of Cu2+, the I 512/I 617 fluorescence intensity ratio was lower compared to that of the system with His only. No significant variation in the I 512/I 617 fluorescence intensity ratio were found when Cu2+ was pretreated with cysteine for 15 min, as shown in Fig. S9. Cysteine can snatch Cu2+ from the system owing to its strong affinity toward Cu2+ [35,36]. In order to avoid the influence of Cu2+ toward His determination, cysteine was chosen as the reagent to mask Cu2+ with satisfactory effects.

The feasibility of using the as-prepared sensor to determine His in real samples was assessed further by determining His in spiked real human urine samples. The His concentration of 0.64 mM in the urine samples was consistent with the normal concentration range of 0.18–1.2 mM for human urine. The recoveries of the His added to the urine samples were 97.77%–100.1%, and the relative standard deviation for three replicate samples was < 4.0% (Table 1 ). This indicated that the CD/calcein/Ni2+ system is a promising fluorescence probe for determining His in biological samples.

We successfully prepared a simple and label-free CD/calcein/Ni2+ complex for use as a fluorescent probe for determining His. The probe works by taking advantage of the well-known affinity between Ni2+ and His. The fluorescent probe gave two fluorescence emission peaks (512 and 617 nm) when excited using a single wavelength. The intensity of the calcein/Ni2+ fluorescence emission peak at 512 nm was markedly enhanced when His was added but the intensity of the CD fluorescence emission peak at 617 nm remained unchanged. The fluorescence intensity ratio had a good linear relationship with the His concentration in the range 0.5–22 μM, and the detection limit was 0.16 μM. The CD/calcein/Ni2+ probe is selective for His in the presence of other natural amino acids and allows His to be determined in urine with a good degree of sensitivity and without the need for a masking agent. The experimental results indicated that the fluorescent probe is a promising candidate for the ratiometric determination of His in biological samples.

Jinshui Liu: Conceptualization, Data curation, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Bo Song: Data curation, Formal analysis, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Ting Fu: Data curation, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province, China (1708085MB48).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.121951.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

Microchim. Acta

Acta, 187 (2020).
